content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660              322-2901           322-6441              322-6444

December 14, 1982


Richard Oberholzer
1501 Trustun Avenue
Bakersfield, CA 93301

                                        Re: Our No. A-82-182

Dear Mr. Oberholzer:

     Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of Mr. Tom
Payne of the Bakersfield City Council. The facts, as I
understand them, are that, prior to April 1982, Mr. Payne owned
100% of Watson Realty Company, which operates in a leased
building located in the City's redevelopment area. In April, he
sold 20% of the business to Carco, a corporation, and, in June
1982, Carco acquired Mr. Payne's remaining interest in the
company. Carco gave Mr. Payne a promissory note for the money
owed on the sale and used its stock interest in Watson Company
as collateral. In case of default, Watson Company may be
liquidated and, if so, once three other creditors are satisfied,
any remaining money would be used to pay the debt owed to
Mr. Payne.

     Mr. Payne is now working as a real estate broker. He pays
Watson Company for office space, a telephone, maintenance and
advertising, but he does not get any client referrals from the
company, nor does he share any of his commissions with Watson.
In the past Mr. Payne handled real estate transactions on
property located in the redevelopment area; however, he has now
decided to confine his future real estate activities to areas
outside of the area.

     Mr. Payne wants to know how the conflict of interests
provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to his situation
and whether he may participate, as a councilmember, in decisions
concerning the redevelopment area.

Richard Oberholzer
December 14, 1982
Page 2


DISCUSSION

A.  Sources of Income

The conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform
Act provide that no local government official shall make or
participate in the making of a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest
(Government Code Section 87100).1/  An official has a
financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally,2/ on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more
than $1,000.

. . .

     (c)  Any source of income . . . aggregating . . .
$250 or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior
to the time when the decision is made.

Section 87103.

Until July 1982, Watson Company was a source of income to
Mr. Payne of $250 or more because of the salary that he
received.  After Mr. Payne sold his interest in Watson Company,
the company ceased to be a source of income to him and the
relationship became that of landlord and tenant.3/

While Mr. Payne had an ownership interest in Watson Company,
his pro rata share of income to the company was considered

_______________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise indicated.

2/  See the enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18703.

3/  The finding of a landlord-tenant relationship is based
upon the facts, as I understand them.

Richard Oberholzer
December 14, 1982
Page 3


to be income to him personally (Section 8203).4/   Thus, if,
during that time, any clients provided the company with
sufficient gross income, such that Mr. Payne's pro rata share of
the income from the client was $250 or more, then the client is
also a source of income to Mr. Payne.

     Currently, Mr. Payne is acquiring additional sources of
income as a real estate broker because he receives commissions
from each sale and lease that he completes.  The source of this
income depends upon whom Mr. Payne represents in the transaction
(for instance, it can be the buyer, the seller, or both
parties).

     Finally, Carco will become a source of income to Mr. Payne
when it begins repaying the promissory note (Section 8203D).
Mr. Payne will be subject to disqualification on certain
decisions, affecting Carco for 12 months after receiving the
last installment payment.

B.   Investment Interests

     Prior to June 1982, Mr. Payne had an investment interest in
Watson Company, worth over $1,000.  He has now entirely divested
himself of this interest.  Mr. Payne may, however, possess an
investment interest in Carco because of the promissory note that
the company issued to him.  Section 8203A defines "investment"
to mean "any financial interest in or security issued by a
business entity, including ... debt instruments, worth over
$1,000."  A promissory note is a debt instrument of the company
issuing the note.  Thus, Mr. Payne has an investment interest in
Carco if the fair market value of the note is over $1,000.

C.   Disqualification

     Mr. Payne is required to disqualify himself from making or
participating in the making of a redevelopment decision5/ if

_______________________
4/  Section 8203D states that income of an individual
includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity
in which the individual owns a 10% or greater interest.

     Mr. Payne's pro rata share of the income received by the
company was either 80% or 100%, depending on when the income ws
received.

5/  Or any other governmental decision.

Richard Oberholzer
December 14, 1982
Page 4


it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on a source of income to him of $250
or more received within the 12 months prior to a decision, or on
a business entity in which he holds an investment interest worth
over $1,000.

     To determine whether a decision will materially affect a
source of income that is a business entity,5/ apply the test
articulated in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. The same tests
are used to determine whether a decision will materially affect
a business entity in which an official has an investment
interest of over $1,000.7/ Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702 provides that a decision's effect is material if
it will increase or decrease:

     (A)  The annualized gross revenues by the lessor
of:

          1.  $100,000; or

          2.  1% if the effect is $1,000 or more; or

     (B)  Annual net income by the lessor of:

          1.  $50,000; or

          2.  1/2% if the effect is $1,000 or more; or

     (C)  Current assets or liabilities by the lessor
of:

          1.  $100,000; or

          2.  1/2% if the effect is $1,000 or more.

                         2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
                         18702(b)(3)(C).

     Applying these tests to decisions affecting Watson Company,
it appears that there are primarily two types of redevelopment

_________________
     6/  Watson Company, Carco and, those clients of Mr. Payne
who are business entities.

     7/  Carco, if the note issued by the company is worth over
$1,000.

Richard Oberholzer
December 14, 1982
Page 5


decisions that may have a material financial effect on the
company.  The first type are decisions which will affect the
company's lease or the value of the lease.  The second type are
decisions which will have an impact on the company's income by
affecting specific clients of the company, or transactions that
the company is engaged in.

If the source of income affected by a decision is not a
business entity,8/ an effect on the source is considered to be
material if it will be "significant."  (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(3)(D).)  This determination is based upon all of the
circumstances surrounding the decision.  If you are confronted
with any situations in which you cannot decide whether
disqualification is necessary, please feel free to contact our
office for advice.

You indicated that, in the future, Mr. Payne has decided to
only handle real estate transactions for land located outside of
the redevelopment area.  Ultimately this may increase the number
of redevelopment decisions in which he will be able to
participate because fewer sources of income will have interests
in the redevelopment area that can be materially affected by the
redevelopment decisions.  However, the fact that the land
transactions are located outside of the redevelopment area does
not mean that Mr. Payne will not be confronted with
redevelopment decisions affecting his sources of income, for
they may have other interests which may be affected by these
decisions.

I hope I have answered all of Mr. Payne's questions.  If you
have any further questions, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JS:Mpln
cc:  Mr. Thomas Payne
Enclosures


8/  For example, clients of Mr. Payne who are individuals.

OFFICE OF CITY ATTORNEY

ARTHUR J. SAALFIELD
Assistant City Attorney

RICHARD J. OBERHOLZER
City Attorney

J. DEAN RICE
Deputy City Attorney

1501 TRUXTUN AVENUE
BAKERSFIELD, CALIFORNIA 93301

CONDIE THOMAS SWALLOW
Deputy City Attorney

Telephone (805) 326-3721

ROBERT M. SHERFY
Deputy City Attorney

JERRY WEBER
Claims Administrator

TERRELL W. KINNEY
City Attorney Investigator

October 25, 1982

Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

Gentlemen:

     Pursuant to section 83114 (b) of the California Government Code, this office is requesting your written advice regarding whether a conflict exists for a City Councilmember while participating in redevelopment matters.

     The facts are as follows:  Councilman Tom Payne previously owned the Watson Company, a real estate business with its office located within the Redevelopment Project Area.  The Watson Company has previously dealt as real estate agent in the sale, lease, etc., of property within the Redevelopment Project Area.  While Councilman Payne owned the Watson Company, he did not participate in any discussion or vote on matters dealing with redevelopment projects.

     Councilman Payne has now sold the Watson Company.  As part of the sale price, Mr. Payne was given a non-secured personal note from the purchasers.  Mr. Payne also retained the right to reacquire the Watson Company from a third party (U.S. Home Corp.), which holds the stock in Watson Company, in the event that the purchasers default.  Councilman Payne desires to continue as an independent real estate broker, working for Watson Company.  As an independent broker, he will receive no direct salary from the Watson Company, but does receive a commission on each sale he makes.  A portion of the commission goes to the Watson Company, in exchange for which the Watson Company will provide Mr. Payne with office space, telephone, maintenance and advertising.  Mr. Payne says that he will not engage in the sale or listing of any property within, or in close proximity to, the Redevelopment Project Area.  Will Councilman Payne have a conflict if he votes on redevelopment matters?

Fair Political Practices Commission
October 25, 1982
Page 2

If Councilman Payne has a conflict under the above
circumstances, will he continue to have a conflict if he receives
full payment for the sale of the Watson Company, but continues
to work as an independent real estate broker out of the Watson
Company office pursuant to the terms previously described?

Your assistance in this matter is appreciated. If you
have any question, you are welcome to contact this office.

Very truly yours,

Richard J. Oberholzer
City Attorney

RJO/bl

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5562          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444


November 2, 1982


Richard J. Oberholzer
Bakersfield City Attorney
1501 Truxton Avenue
Bakersfield, CA 93301

                                                                Re: A-82-182

Dear Mr. Oberholzer:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:plh
Enclosure

sections.question: Mr. Payne wants to know how the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to his situation and whether he may participate, as a councilmember, in decisions concerning the redevelopment area. The specific questions are: (1) Will Councilman Payne have a conflict if he votes on redevelopment matters? (2) If Councilman Payne has a conflict under the described circumstances, will he continue to have a conflict if he receives full payment for the sale of the Watson Company, but continues to work as an independent real estate broker out of the watson Company office?
sections.conclusion: Mr. Payne is required to disqualify himself from making or participating in the making of a redevelopment decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on a source of income to him of $250 or more received within the 12 months prior to a decision, or on a business entity in which he holds an investment interest worth over $1,000. Sources of income include: (1) until July 1982, Watson Company as a source of income; (2) currently, income from real estate brokerage commissions; and (3) future income from Carco as it repays the promissory note. The determination of whether a decision will materially affect his sources of income depends on application of specific tests regarding changes in revenues, net income, and assets. His decision to confine future real estate activities outside the redevelopment area may increase the number of redevelopment decisions in which he can participate.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a Bakersfield City Councilmember who previously owned a real estate company with operations in the city's redevelopment area, and who is now divesting his ownership interest while continuing as an independent broker, has a conflict of interest that would require him to disqualify himself from redevelopment decisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Payne must disqualify himself from redevelopment decisions that would materially affect his sources of income or investments over $1,000, specifically regarding Watson Company's lease and clients. Future decisions about redevelopment matters may allow greater participation if his real estate activities and sources of income have no material interest in redevelopment area transactions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (October 25, 1982) from City Attorney Richard Oberholzer and the FPPC response letter (December 14, 1982) from Counsel Janis Shank McLean. The response contains detailed DISCUSSION section with subsections on Sources of Income, Investment Interests, and Disqualification. Extraction confidence is high; minor uncertainty only regarding some interpretive statements in the conclusion section.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Mr. Payne wants to know how the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to his situation and whether he may participate, as a councilmember, in decisions concerning the redevelopment area. The specific questions are: (1) Will Councilman Payne have a conflict if he votes on redevelopment matters? (2) If Councilman Payne has a conflict under the described circumstances, will he continue to have a conflict if he receives full payment for the sale of the Watson Company, but continues to work as an independent real estate broker out of the watson Company office?

CONCLUSION: Mr. Payne is required to disqualify himself from making or participating in the making of a redevelopment decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on a source of income to him of $250 or more received within the 12 months prior to a decision, or on a business entity in which he holds an investment interest worth over $1,000. Sources of income include: (1) until July 1982, Watson Company as a source of income; (2) currently, income from real estate brokerage commissions; and (3) future income from Carco as it repays the promissory note. The determination of whether a decision will materially affect his sources of income depends on application of specific tests regarding changes in revenues, net income, and assets. His decision to confine future real estate activities outside the redevelopment area may increase the number of redevelopment decisions in which he can participate.

The facts, as I understand them, are that, prior to April 1982, Mr. Payne owned 100% of Watson Realty Company, which operates in a leased building located in the City's redevelopment area. In April, he sold 20% of the business to Carco, a corporation, and, in June 1982, Carco acquired Mr. Payne's remaining interest in the company. Carco gave Mr. Payne a promissory note for the money owed on the sale and used its stock interest in Watson Company as collateral. In case of default, Watson Company may be liquidated and, if so, once three other creditors are satisfied, any remaining money would be used to pay the debt owed to Mr. Payne. Sources of Income The conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act provide that no local government official shall make or participate in the making of a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest (Government Code Section 87100).1/ An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,2/ on: (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000. . . . embedding.summary: The FPPC provides advice on whether Bakersfield City Councilmember Tom Payne has a conflict of interest in redevelopment matters after selling his real estate company. The analysis determines that disqualification is required for decisions that would materially affect his sources of income (Watson Company, brokerage commissions, and promissory note from Carco) or his investment in Carco, with application of specific financial thresholds to determine materiality.
